editorial.bodyTr: The Democratic Party and People Power Party are engaged in a tense struggle over the distribution of standing committee chairs in negotiations to organize the 22nd National Assembly. editorial.titleTr: [Editorial] Samsung Tentative Deal: Now It's Time to Set Principles, Not Just Patch Things Up
summaryTr: Samsung Tentative Agreement: Now Time to Establish Principles Beyond Patching Things Up Samsung Electronics labor and management reached a tentative compromise agreement ahead of a general strike.
Both labor and management signed off on the tentative agreement after continuing late-night negotiations mediated by the Minister of Labor.
The union will call off a strike scheduled for the 21st and hold a vote on the tentative agreement.
editorial.bodyTr:
Samsung's tentative agreement: now we must establish principles beyond temporary fixes

Samsung Electronics' labor and management have reached a tentative compromise as a general strike loomed. Both sides signed the tentative agreement after continuing late-night negotiations under the mediation of the Minister of Labor. The union has halted the strike scheduled for the 21st and agreed to submit the tentative agreement to a vote by union members.

This is the result of both sides stepping back from the brink of a massive labor dispute that would have paralyzed the company's operations. The agreement was reached after weeks of negotiations, and it reflects compromises on both sides—management's concessions on wages and working conditions, and the union's acceptance of reasonable operational needs.

However, the fact that Samsung's labor relations continue to be resolved through last-minute emergency measures and government mediation raises a larger question about the company's labor management philosophy. What Samsung truly needs now is not a temporary fix that merely prevents crisis, but a fundamental shift toward establishing clear principles in labor relations.

The tentative agreement may resolve this round of conflict, but it does not address the root cause: the lack of a shared understanding between labor and management about what fair labor relations should look like. Without establishing such principles, Samsung will likely face repeated conflicts over the same issues.

Both Samsung management and the union must recognize that genuine stability in labor relations comes not from avoiding disputes, but from building trust through transparent communication and principled negotiations. The company should use this opportunity to develop a comprehensive labor policy that both sides can respect and follow consistently.

editorials.titleTr: Editorial: With Interest Rates Rising, Household Debt Hits 2 Trillion Won—Proactive Risk Management Urgent
summaryTr: Household loans reached a record high of 1,993 trillion won at the end of March.
Jeonse loans and debt-fueled investments are the primary drivers of surging household debt.
Proactive risk management is essential given the deteriorating quality of debt.
editorials.bodyTr:
Household Debt Reaches 2 Trillion Won Amid Rising Interest Rates, Preemptive Risk Management Urgent

Household loans hit a record high of 1,993 trillion won at the end of March. This is the combined result of people stretching their finances to purchase homes and investors borrowing money to invest in stocks. More concerning is the deteriorating quality of debt. Bank lending has declined slightly due to government regulations, but household borrowing from non-bank financial institutions has surged. Precarious loans with high interest rates are expanding rapidly. As the central bank continues its rate-hiking cycle to combat inflation, the burden on households with excessive debt is mounting. The household debt problem, already serious, could spiral into a systemic financial risk if left unchecked. The government and financial authorities must shift from passive regulation to proactive risk management. First, it is necessary to strengthen monitoring of non-bank lending and tighten standards for high-risk loans. Second, financial education for vulnerable households should be expanded to prevent reckless borrowing. Third, structural measures are needed to ease housing market pressures that drive people to overextend themselves. Without decisive action now, the mounting debt crisis could trigger broader economic instability.

editorial.body: 입력 종합 26면 삼성전자 초기업노조의 총파업이 유보됐다. 총파업을 하루 앞두고 열린 중앙노동위원회 3차 사후조정에 이어 김영훈 고용노동부 장관의 비공식 중재를 통한 막판 반전이다. 피해액 규모가 100조원에 이를 것이란 전망까지 나오던 상황을 파국 일보 직전에 막았다는 점에서 다행스러운 일이다. 성과급 재원과 특별보상 기간 등에선 일부 절충이 있었지만, 적자를 내는 사업부까지 억대 성과급을 배분하라는 삼성전자 초기업노조의 요구가 걸림돌이었으나 잠정합의안을 조합원 투표에 부치기로 했다. 이재명 대통령도 어제 국무회의에서 “세금도 떼기 전에 영업이익의 일정 비율을 제도적으로 나눠 갖는 것은 투자자도 할 수 없는 일”이라며 “노조 단체행동도 적정한 선이 있어야 한다”고 노조의 파업 철회를 압박했다. 초격차를 위협받고 있는 삼성전자의 총파업은 상상도 할 수 없는 일이다. 일부 핵심 공정은 필수 인력이 유지하겠지만, 18일간 파업이 현실화하면 삼성전자의 글로벌 신뢰는 치명타를 입게 된다. 삼성 내부에서는 “엔비디아가 파업 기간 생산된 제품은 받지 않겠다고 했다”는 이야기까지 나돌았다. 파업 자체만으로 시장 신뢰가 흔들린다는 뜻이다. 공급 안정성에 의문이 생기면 고객사들은 곧바로 대만·중국 기업으로 거래처를 돌릴 수밖에 없다. 삼성전자 매출은 국내총생산(GDP)의 12.5% 수준이다. 생산 차질이 현실화하면 공급망과 수출, 투자 심리까지 연쇄 충격이 불가피하다. 정부가 피해 규모를 최대 100조원까지 우려했던 이유다. 일단 위기를 넘겼지만 과제는 여전히 많다. 초격차 유지를 위한 투자 확대 등 성장 전략 재정비는 기본이고, 어수선해진 내부 분위기를 추스리는 것도 시급해졌다. 반도체(DS)부문 주도의 성과급 협상에 반발해 온 TV·가전 등 디바이스경험(DX)부문 조합원들은 교섭중단 가처분까지 신청한 상황이다. 삼성전자 협력업체 노조들도 성과 배분에 목소리를 내기 시작했다. 삼성전자 초기업노조가 불을 붙이면서 현대차·HD현대중공업 등 주요 대기업에서도 ‘영업이익 N% 성과급’ 요구가 확산하는 분위기다. 합리적 성과 배분 체계도 구축해야 한다. 글로벌 반도체 기업들이 채택하는 양도금지조건부주식(RSU)이 대표적이다. 이 제도는 회사가 성장하는 만큼 보상이 지속되므로 인재의 유지와 신규 확보에 유리하다. 삼성전자 초기업노조도 성찰이 필요하다. 회사의 경영 판단 영역인 성과급까지 미리 요구하는 것은 황금알을 낳는 거위의 배를 갈라 돈잔치하는 것이다. 이기주의에서 벗어나 연대와 상생의 노동조합 초심으로 돌아가야 한다.

editorial.titleTr: Samsung Union Must Pass Tentative Deal Vote to End Strike
summaryTr: Samsung Electronics' initial union announces postponement of general strike, as reported on page 26 of the comprehensive input section.

This marks a last-minute reversal following the third post-adjustment session at the Central Labor Relations Commission held a day before the planned strike, along with informal mediation by Employment and Labor Minister Kim Young-hoon.
It is fortunate that the situation, which had faced forecasts of potential damage reaching 100 trillion won, was averted just before catastrophe.
editorial.bodyTr: Samsung Electronics' initial company-wide union has suspended its planned general strike. This is a dramatic last-minute turnaround following the third post-settlement mediation session at the Central Labor Relations Commission held one day before the strike, followed by informal mediation efforts by Minister of Employment and Labor Kim Young-hoon. It is fortunate that the situation, which had been expected to result in losses exceeding 100 trillion won, was averted at the brink of catastrophe. While there was some compromise on performance bonus funding and special compensation periods, the stumbling block had been the initial company union's demand to distribute hundreds of millions of won in performance bonuses even to loss-making business divisions. The union has now agreed to put a tentative agreement to a member vote. President Lee Jae-myung also pressured the union to withdraw the strike during yesterday's cabinet meeting, saying "taking a certain percentage of operating profit as a system before even deducting taxes is something even investors cannot do" and "union collective actions must also have reasonable limits." A general strike at Samsung Electronics, whose technological leadership is already under threat, is unthinkable. While essential personnel will maintain certain critical processes, if an 18-day strike becomes reality, Samsung Electronics' global credibility will suffer a fatal blow. Inside Samsung, there are even reports that "NVIDIA said it will not accept products manufactured during the strike period." This shows how much market confidence can be shaken by the mere threat of a strike. Once supply stability comes into question, clients will have no choice but to switch their business to Taiwanese and Chinese companies. Samsung Electronics' sales account for 12.5 percent of gross domestic product. If production disruptions materialize, cascading shocks to the supply chain, exports, and investment sentiment are inevitable. This is why the government was concerned about losses reaching up to 100 trillion won. While the immediate crisis has been averted, there remain many challenges. Beyond basic restructuring of growth strategies, including expanded investment to maintain technological leadership, there is an urgent need to restore order to an unsettled internal atmosphere. Members of the Device Experience division, which includes television and home appliances, who have opposed negotiations dominated by the semiconductor division, have even filed for an injunction to suspend negotiations. Subcontractor unions at Samsung Electronics are also beginning to voice demands for profit sharing. As the initial company union ignited the issue, major conglomerates including Hyundai Motor and HD Hyundai Heavy Industries are seeing a spreading atmosphere of demands for "operating profit-based N percent performance bonuses." A more rational profit-sharing system must also be established. The restricted stock unit program adopted by global semiconductor companies is a prime example. This system ensures continued compensation as the company grows, making it advantageous for talent retention and recruitment. Samsung Electronics' initial company union also needs to reflect. Demanding performance bonuses, which fall within the company's management prerogative, beforehand is like killing the goose that lays the golden egg for a brief feast. The union must return to its original principles of solidarity and mutual prosperity, leaving behind narrow self-interest.

editorial.title: Beyond a bonus dispute
editorial.body: Samsung Electronics conflict underscores Korea's widening labor market inequality Workers in South Korea do not all face the same circumstances. Opinions are split over the recent labor unions’ fights for performance bonuses at some big companies, depending on workers’ company size, age and job category. In particular, workers at smaller firms feel relatively deprived by the massive performance bonuses being demanded by unions at Samsung Electronics and SK hynix. The controversy surrounding performance bonuses at these companies resonates more sensitively with many workers because the size of the bonuses is far removed from their own wage levels. Data from the National Tax Service’s 2024 earned-income tax filings showed that the average salary of South Korean workers came to approximately 45 million won ($29,800) annually, or 3.75 million won on a monthly basis. Yet the median annual salary — the midpoint in the income distribution — stood at only 34.17 million won, or 2.85 million won a month. The average exceeds the median by 10.83 million won. These figures indicate that a small number of high-wage earners are driving up the average so that most workers earn much less than the statistical average. In this situation, the performance bonuses sought by the union at Samsung Electronics are bound to deepen the sense of disconnect felt by many workers. Demands by unions at large corporations for performance bonuses that small and medium-sized company workers would struggle to earn even after decades of work are likely to be seen as greed on the part of high-income big-business unions, making them difficult for most people to sympathize with. Especially, if a model of tying performance bonuses to a fixed share of operating profits takes root at major industry players like Samsung Electronics, the compensation gap between large corporations and smaller companies unable to sustain such a system will inevitably expand. The 2024 results on income from wage and salary jobs, released by the Ministry of Data and Statistics, showed that the average monthly pay for employees at large firms reached 6.13 million won, about twice that of employees at small and medium-sized companies, who earned 3.07 million won on average. Wages at large corporations rose 3.3 percent from a year earlier, while those at small and medium-sized companies increased only 3.0 percent. The gap may widen still further as big-company unions demand a fixed share of operating profits as performance bonuses. Especially by age, the difference widened sharply: 1.21 million won among workers in their 20s, 2.44 million won among those in their 30s, 3.93 million won for workers in their 40s and 4.56 million won for those in their 50s. The divide between regular and non-regular employees is also becoming larger. According to the Ministry of Employment and Labor, the hourly wages of non-regular workers fell for two consecutive years, reaching only 65.2 percent of regular employees' pay last year, the biggest shortfall in 11 years. This means that a non-regular worker earned only 652,000 won for every 1 million won earned by a regular employee. Last year, the hourly wage of regular workers rose 3.2 percent from a year earlier to 28,599 won, while that of nonregular workers increased only 1.3 percent to 18,635 won. Even allowing for the fact that many non-regular workers are older or work shorter hours, the imbalance is still severe. At a time when pay and benefit gaps between large corporations and smaller firms are already widening, if disparities in performance bonuses also expand based on company size rather than individual achievement, the dual labor market will deteriorate to an irreversible level. Unless the severe inequality problem is tackled, both social unity and economic growth will be at risk. Solutions should be sought to keep social conflict from worsening further. The labor dispute at Samsung Electronics over record operating profits generated by the AI semiconductor super-boom is prompting a broader rethinking of Korea's overall labor market, beyond pay conflict. summaryTr: 삼성의 보너스 분쟁이 한국의 대기업과 중소기업 간 격차 심화를 드러냈다.
대기업 근로자들은 중소기업 근로자의 2배 연봉을 받으며, 나이 많은 직원들 사이에서 격차가 가장 빠르게 벌어지고 있다.
이익과 연동된 성과급 모델은 이원적 노동시장을 돌이킬 수 없는 수준까지 심화시킬 위험이 있다.
editorial.bodyTr:
삼성전자 노사분쟁이 드러낸 한국의 노동시장 불평등 확대

한국의 근로자들이 모두 같은 상황에 처해 있지는 않다. 최근 대기업의 성과급을 놓고 노조가 벌인 투쟁에 대한 의견이 갈리는데, 이는 근로자의 회사 규모, 나이, 직종에 따라 달라진다. 특히 중소기업 근로자들은 삼성전자와 SK하이닉스 노조가 요구하는 막대한 성과급으로 인해 상대적 박탈감을 느끼고 있다. 이들 회사의 성과급을 둘러싼 논란이 많은 근로자들에게 더욱 민감하게 와 닿는 이유는 성과급의 규모가 그들의 임금 수준과 거리가 멀기 때문이다.

국세청의 2024년 근로소득 세무신고 자료에 따르면 한국 근로자의 평균 연봉은 약 4,500만원으로, 월평균 약 375만원에 달한다. 그러나 중위 연봉은 소득 분포의 중간값으로 3,417만원, 즉 월 285만원에 불과했다. 평균이 중위값을 1,083만원 상회한다. 이는 극히 소수의 고소득층이 평균을 끌어올려 대다수 근로자가 통계 평균보다 훨씬 적게 벌고 있음을 시사한다. 이러한 상황에서 삼성전자 노조가 요구하는 성과급은 많은 근로자들이 느끼는 괴리감을 심화시킬 수밖에 없다. 대기업 노조가 중소기업 근로자가 몇십 년을 일해도 벌기 힘든 규모의 성과급을 요구하는 것은 고소득 대기업 노조의 탐욕으로 비춰져 대다수 국민의 공감을 얻기 어렵다. 특히 삼성전자 같은 주요 산업의 플레이어에서 성과급을 영업이익의 일정 비율로 연동하는 모델이 뿌리내린다면, 이러한 체계를 유지할 수 없는 중소기업과의 임금 격차는 필연적으로 확대될 것이다.

통계청이 발표한 2024년 임금근로 소득 결과에 따르면 대기업 근로자의 월평균 임금은 613만원으로 중소기업 근로자의 307만원의 약 2배에 달했다. 대기업 임금은 전년 대비 3.3% 상승했고, 중소기업은 3.0% 증가하는 데 그쳤다. 대기업 노조가 영업이익의 일정 비율을 성과급으로 요구함에 따라 격차가 더 벌어질 가능성이 높다.

특히 연령별로는 격차가 급격히 벌어졌다. 20대 근로자는 121만원, 30대는 244만원, 40대는 393만원, 50대는 456만원의 격차를 보였다. 정규직과 비정규직 간 격차도 커지고 있다. 고용노동부에 따르면 비정규직 근로자의 시급은 2년 연속 하락해 정규직의 65.2% 수준에 머물렀으며, 이는 11년 만에 최악의 수치다. 이는 정규직 근로자가 100만원을 벌 때 비정규직 근로자는 65만2,000원만 버는 것을 의미한다. 지난해 정규직 근로자의 시급은 전년 대비 3.2% 상승해 28,599원이 됐고, 비정규직 근로자는 1.3% 증가한 18,635원에 그쳤다. 비정규직 근로자 대다수가 고령이거나 단시간 근무를 한다는 점을 감안하더라도 불균형은 여전히 심각하다.

대기업과 중소기업 간 임금과 복리후생 격차가 이미 벌어지고 있는 상황에서, 성과급 격차까지 회사 규모에 따라 확대된다면 이중 노동시장은 되돌릴 수 없는 수준으로 악화될 것이다. 이러한 심각한 불평등 문제를 해결하지 않으면 사회적 결집과 경제 성장이 위험에 처할 것이다. 사회 갈등이 더 심화되는 것을 막기 위한 해결책이 모색돼야 한다. 인공지능 반도체 호황으로 기록적인 영업이익을 기록한 삼성전자의 임금 분쟁은 임금 갈등을 넘어 한국의 전체 노동시장에 대한 폭넓은 재검토를 촉구하고 있다.

editorials.title: Beyond a bonus dispute
editorials.body: Samsung Electronics conflict underscores Korea's widening labor market inequality Workers in South Korea do not all face the same circumstances. Opinions are split over the recent labor unions’ fights for performance bonuses at some big companies, depending on workers’ company size, age and job category. In particular, workers at smaller firms feel relatively deprived by the massive performance bonuses being demanded by unions at Samsung Electronics and SK hynix. The controversy surrounding performance bonuses at these companies resonates more sensitively with many workers because the size of the bonuses is far removed from their own wage levels. Data from the National Tax Service’s 2024 earned-income tax filings showed that the average salary of South Korean workers came to approximately 45 million won ($29,800) annually, or 3.75 million won on a monthly basis. Yet the median annual salary — the midpoint in the income distribution — stood at only 34.17 million won, or 2.85 million won a month. The average exceeds the median by 10.83 million won. These figures indicate that a small number of high-wage earners are driving up the average so that most workers earn much less than the statistical average. In this situation, the performance bonuses sought by the union at Samsung Electronics are bound to deepen the sense of disconnect felt by many workers. Demands by unions at large corporations for performance bonuses that small and medium-sized company workers would struggle to earn even after decades of work are likely to be seen as greed on the part of high-income big-business unions, making them difficult for most people to sympathize with. Especially, if a model of tying performance bonuses to a fixed share of operating profits takes root at major industry players like Samsung Electronics, the compensation gap between large corporations and smaller companies unable to sustain such a system will inevitably expand. The 2024 results on income from wage and salary jobs, released by the Ministry of Data and Statistics, showed that the average monthly pay for employees at large firms reached 6.13 million won, about twice that of employees at small and medium-sized companies, who earned 3.07 million won on average. Wages at large corporations rose 3.3 percent from a year earlier, while those at small and medium-sized companies increased only 3.0 percent. The gap may widen still further as big-company unions demand a fixed share of operating profits as performance bonuses. Especially by age, the difference widened sharply: 1.21 million won among workers in their 20s, 2.44 million won among those in their 30s, 3.93 million won for workers in their 40s and 4.56 million won for those in their 50s. The divide between regular and non-regular employees is also becoming larger. According to the Ministry of Employment and Labor, the hourly wages of non-regular workers fell for two consecutive years, reaching only 65.2 percent of regular employees' pay last year, the biggest shortfall in 11 years. This means that a non-regular worker earned only 652,000 won for every 1 million won earned by a regular employee. Last year, the hourly wage of regular workers rose 3.2 percent from a year earlier to 28,599 won, while that of nonregular workers increased only 1.3 percent to 18,635 won. Even allowing for the fact that many non-regular workers are older or work shorter hours, the imbalance is still severe. At a time when pay and benefit gaps between large corporations and smaller firms are already widening, if disparities in performance bonuses also expand based on company size rather than individual achievement, the dual labor market will deteriorate to an irreversible level. Unless the severe inequality problem is tackled, both social unity and economic growth will be at risk. Solutions should be sought to keep social conflict from worsening further. The labor dispute at Samsung Electronics over record operating profits generated by the AI semiconductor super-boom is prompting a broader rethinking of Korea's overall labor market, beyond pay conflict. editorial.titleTr: [Editorial] Samsung Electronics Deal a Relief, but Many Challenges Remain
summaryTr: 삼성전자 노사가 성과급 협상에서 극적 합의를 도출했다.
파업 직전 노사 양보와 정부 중재로 극적 타결을 이뤘다.
하지만 해결해야 할 과제들이 여전히 남아있는 상황이다.
editorial.bodyTr: Samsung Electronics management and union have reached a tentative agreement on performance bonuses. With the strike deadline that the union had announced fast approaching, there was considerable public concern, but reaching a dramatic last-minute agreement is a welcome outcome. The result reflects mutual concessions from both labor and management, as well as government mediation efforts right up until the end. Samsung Electronics' labor and management have avoided a strike.

파업 직전 노사 양보와 정부 중재로 극적 타결을 이뤘다.

editorials.bodyTr: Samsung Electronics management and union have reached a tentative agreement on performance bonuses. With the strike deadline that the union had announced fast approaching, there was considerable public concern, but reaching a dramatic last-minute agreement is a welcome outcome. The result reflects mutual concessions from both labor and management, as well as government mediation efforts right up until the end. Samsung Electronics' labor and management have avoided a strike.

Aftereffects from compromising management principles appear inevitable.
editorial.bodyTr: Samsung Electronics management and labor have reached a tentative agreement just hours before a scheduled general strike. While the ratification vote by union members remains pending, the prospect of avoiding a strike has significantly increased. However, aftereffects appear unavoidable. There are concerns that the government's anxiousness to prevent a strike at all costs has compromised fundamental management principles.
